title: podman restore failed when migrating to another host : exited (132)
body:
### Issue Description

Hi,

I tried jupyter notebook in container migrates to another host using podman.
The testing host is AWS on-demand t2 and t3.

When starting the container and checkpoint at t2, migrating to t3 is a success, and t2 -> t3 -> t2 migration testing is a success.
However, t3 -> t2 migration is failed

Here are the commands I used.

// create a container
sudo podman run -p 8888:8888 -e GRATN_SUDO=yes --user root --name jupynb docker.io/jupyter/base-notebook start-notebook.sh --NotebookNotary.db_file=':memory:'

// checkpoint
sudo podman container checkpoint --file-locks --tcp-established --keep --export dump.tar.gz jupynb

// restore
sudo podman container restore --file-locks --tcp-established --keep --import dump.tar.gz

Why fail to restore? 

I apologize for my poor English.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Start AWS EC2 Instance(t2, t3)
2. Run container at t3(Execute the commands I used)
3. Migrate t3 to t2 and restore from t2 instance(execute the command used)


### Describe the results you received

[ec2-user@t2-rhel9 podman]$ sudo podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                                   COMMAND               CREATED            STATUS                          PORTS                   NAMES
e97e6e497596  docker.io/jupyter/base-notebook:latest  start-notebook.sh...  About an hour ago  Exited (132) About an hour ago  0.0.0.0:8888->8888/tcp  jupynb

---

When I started that container, the memory was gone. I want to preserve the memory.

### Describe the results you expected

restored container state is running.

In case of migrating from t2 to t3, it works as I want.

Container image : docker.io/jupyter/base-notebook

### Additional information

I suspect the CPU support issue due to the following cases.

[Docker containers exit code 132](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/60930359/docker-containers-exit-code-132)

[Docker container exited with code 132](https://forums.docker.com/t/docker-container-exited-with-code-132/113173)

comments.body: @adrianreber does the user need to do anything to preserve the memory?

@limjunho The log file you showed looks like CRIU restored the container successfully.

Can you see something that the process crashed in your logs (or dmesg).

If your process in the container is using features of the CPU which the destination does not have the migration can fail. From the information you have collected I would say, that CRIU was restoring the process successfully, but because it uses CPU features which do not exist on the destination the process might have crashed.

Migrating containers has the same limitations as VM migration, if your destination system does not have features your VM or container is using the migration will  not work.

You can migrate to a CPU with more features, but not to a CPU which does not have a certain feature you are using.

The low level tool doing the checkpointing (CRIU) has the possibility to check for CPU compatibility: https://criu.org/Cpuinfo

But just from looking at the CPU flags of both systems it seems obvious that this might be problematic.

> @limjunho The log file you showed looks like CRIU restored the container successfully.
> 
> Can you see something that the process crashed in your logs (or dmesg).
> 
> If your process in the container is using features of the CPU which the destination does not have the migration can fail. From the information you have collected I would say, that CRIU was restoring the process successfully, but because it uses CPU features which do not exist on the destination the process might have crashed.
> 
> Migrating containers has the same limitations as VM migration, if your destination system does not have features your VM or container is using the migration will not work.
> 
> You can migrate to a CPU with more features, but not to a CPU which does not have a certain feature you are using.
> 
> The low level tool doing the checkpointing (CRIU) has the possibility to check for CPU compatibility: https://criu.org/Cpuinfo
> 
> But just from looking at the CPU flags of both systems it seems obvious that this might be problematic.

I was able to get the following results from the ```criu cpuinfo check``` on the instance that failed to restore.
```
[ec2-user@t2-rhel9 ~]$ sudo criu cpuinfo check
Error (criu/arch/x86/cpu.c:247): cpu: FPU feature required by image is not supported on host (fpu:0 fxsr:0 osxsave:0 xsaves:1)
```
And cpuinfo check of the instance that was successfully restored.
```
[ec2-user@t3-rhel9-t2snap ~]$ sudo criu cpuinfo check
Error (criu/arch/x86/cpu.c:274): cpu: CPU xsave size mismatch (832/2696)
```
I suspected it was a CPU problem, but I couldn't confirm it in detail. Thanks to you, I fully understand my problem and thank you very much for this.

@adrianreber I have some additional questions regarding the CPU compatibility check in CRIU. 

While I know that CRIU has a strict capability mode to check that all CPU features match, it seems that this mode is not always used. Instead, CRIU first conducts compatibility checks at the instruction level, and I'm curious about the x86_ins_capability_mask used for this inspection.

Specifically, I'd like to know why is `x86_ins_capability_mask` defined in criu/blob/criu-dev/criu/arch/x86/cpu.c less than features found in [linux/cpufeatures.h](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/arch/x86/include/asm/cpufeatures.h), and what criteria are used to define the instruction set in x86_ins_capability_mask.

Although I have confirmed in some tests that migration can be successful even if all CPU features do not match. In addition, are there any future plans for compatibility checks for ARM architectures?

> Specifically, I'd like to know why is `x86_ins_capability_mask` defined in criu/blob/criu-dev/criu/arch/x86/cpu.c less than features found in [linux/cpufeatures.h](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/arch/x86/include/asm/cpufeatures.h), and what criteria are used to define the instruction set in x86_ins_capability_mask.

This is a very specific question which you should probably ask over at https://github.com/checkpoint-restore/criu

I cannot answer that.

> Although I have confirmed in some tests that migration can be successful even if all CPU features do not match. In addition, are there any future plans for compatibility checks for ARM architectures?

I am not aware of anybody working on it, but again, better ask that at https://github.com/checkpoint-restore/criu
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we have heard no further feedback, closing.
